figures.gloss: Enlightened human beings who have transcended physical reincarnation to form the Great White Brotherhood. Residing in hidden sanctuaries like Shambhala, they telepathically guide humanity's spiritual evolution and safeguard the primordial esoteric wisdom.
figures.facet: They democratize and secularize the 'prophet' or 'savior.' They are not gods but advanced elder siblings on an evolutionary ladder that every human will eventually climb.
figures.provenance: Blavatsky's 'The Secret Doctrine' (1888) and the 'Mahatma Letters' to A.P. Sinnett, drawing heavily on Tibetan Buddhist Mahasiddhas and Rosicrucian hidden chiefs.

figures.gloss: The subtle, super-sensuous spiritual essence that pervades all space. Within its highest octave reside the 'Akashic Records'—an imperishable, cosmic ledger that records every thought, word, and action that has ever occurred in the universe.
figures.facet: It transforms divine omniscience from the mind of a personal God into a quasi-scientific, universally accessible database of energetic imprints.
figures.provenance: Blavatsky's 'Isis Unveiled' (1877), syncretizing the Vedic concept of Akasha (aether) with Eliphas Levi's 'Astral Light' and 19th-century physics.

figures.gloss: The dynamic, animating energy of the universe, often described as 'cosmic electricity' or the 'fiery whirlwind.' It is the bridge by which the Divine Thought manifests into the material architecture of the cosmos.
figures.facet: It aggressively bridges mysticism and 19th-century electromagnetism, framing divine creation not as a spoken fiat but as a literal, quantifiable electrical current binding spirit to matter.
figures.provenance: 'The Secret Doctrine' (Stanzas of Dzyan), synthesizing the Vedic Agni, the Buddhist concept of kinetic energy (Samskara), and Victorian ether theories.

figures.gloss: Vast, sequential epochs of human evolution, moving from ethereal, ghost-like beings to dense physical bodies like the Atlanteans, and currently progressing back toward spiritualization. Each race develops a specific cosmic sense and faculty.
figures.facet: It explicitly hybridizes Darwinian biological evolution with spiritual emanation, proposing that consciousness evolves before and through physical form across millions of years — a racial-hierarchy framework later mined by Ariosophist and völkisch occultists (Guido von List, Jörg Lanz von Liebenfels) as pseudo-scientific ballast for racial ideology, a liability distinct from, but traceable to, Blavatsky's own text.
figures.provenance: 'The Secret Doctrine', Vol II, blending Hindu Yugas, Hesiod's Ages of Man, and 19th-century race theories and paleontology; its afterlife within Ariosophist and völkisch occultism is documented in Nicholas Goodrick-Clarke's 'The Occult Roots of Nazism' (1985).

figures.gloss: The 'Youth of Sixteen Summers' who descended from Venus millions of years ago to act as the spiritual king and initiator of Earth. He anchors the planetary Logos and resides in the etheric plane of Shambhala.
figures.facet: He divorces planetary rulership from creator-gods. He did not make the Earth; he is simply the highest-ranking spiritual official assigned to manage its cosmic curriculum.
figures.provenance: Charles Webster Leadbeater and Annie Besant's later writings, repurposing the Hindu mind-born sons of Brahma (Kumaras) into an interplanetary hierarchy.

figures.gloss: The personification of intellectual enlightenment and self-consciousness. Rather than a fallen adversary, Lucifer represents the solar energy that awakened dormant human minds during the Third Root Race, gifting humanity the fire of independent thought.
figures.facet: Theosophy systematically rehabilitates the 'Adversary,' defining the Fall not as a sin of disobedience but as the necessary, heroic descent of divine mind into animal matter.
figures.provenance: 'The Secret Doctrine', explicitly identifying the Christian Lucifer with Prometheus and the morning star, completely rejecting his association with Satan.

figures.gloss: The omnipresent, eternal, boundless, and immutable principle that precedes all manifested existence. It is the Unconscious All, completely devoid of attributes, personality, or will.
figures.facet: It radically rejects the Abrahamic personal God. The ultimate reality is emphatically unconscious and impersonal; consciousness only arises as a limitation during cosmic manifestation.
figures.provenance: 'The Secret Doctrine', directly adopting Advaita Vedanta's Nirguna Brahman, mediated through 19th-century Western pantheism.

figures.gloss: The immortal, indivisible core of the human being—the divine spark that journeys through mineral, plant, animal, and human kingdoms to gather experience before returning, fully self-conscious, to the Absolute.
figures.facet: It eliminates the concept of 'created souls.' The human essence is co-eternal with the universe, undergoing an infinitely vast, cyclic pilgrimage of self-becoming.
figures.provenance: Blavatsky's writings, drawing directly on Leibniz's 'Monadology' and the Hindu concepts of Atman and Jiva.

figures.gloss: Russian-born occultist (1831-1891) who co-founded the Theosophical Society in New York in 1875 and authored 'Isis Unveiled' (1877) and 'The Secret Doctrine' (1888), presenting herself as the amanuensis of hidden Masters rather than an independent theorist.
figures.facet: She collapses the distance between prophet and scholar, claiming not personal revelation but stenographic transmission — every doctrine she publishes is framed as received dictation from a higher, verifiable hierarchy.
figures.provenance: Historical founder (1831-1891); biographical detail from Sylvia Cranston's 'HPB' and the Theosophical Society's own foundational records.

figures.gloss: American Civil War veteran, lawyer, and journalist who co-founded the Theosophical Society with Blavatsky in 1875, served as its first president, and moved its headquarters to Adyar, India, in 1882.
figures.facet: His 1880 public conversion to Buddhism in Ceylon and subsequent 'Buddhist Catechism' (1881) made him a genuine agent of Sinhalese Buddhist revival, giving Theosophy a historical footprint independent of Blavatsky's cosmology.
figures.provenance: Historical figure (1832-1907); Olcott's own diaries ('Old Diary Leaves') and the documented Buddhist revival in colonial Ceylon.

figures.gloss: English social reformer, Fabian socialist, and advocate for Indian home rule who succeeded Olcott as the Theosophical Society's second president in 1907, steering it into its most institutionally powerful era.
figures.facet: She fuses Theosophy with practical political activism, running the Society as a global institution while simultaneously certifying its most speculative later doctrine — the coming World Teacher — as literal fact.
figures.provenance: Historical figure (1847-1933); Besant's presidency and the Order of the Star in the East she founded with Leadbeater in 1911.

figures.gloss: Clairvoyant and former Anglican clergyman whose claimed psychic investigations supplied most of Theosophy's second-generation doctrine — chakras, thought-forms, occult chemistry, and the planetary hierarchy around Sanat Kumara.
figures.facet: He replaces textual revelation with a claimed technical instrument: clairvoyant 'investigation' presented with the trappings of laboratory observation, repeatedly destabilized by the sexual scandals that shadowed his career.
figures.provenance: Historical figure (1854-1934); 'Man Visible and Invisible' (1902) and 'The Chakras' (1927); co-authored 'Thought-Forms' (1901) with Besant.

figures.gloss: An Indian boy discovered by Leadbeater at Adyar in 1909 and raised by Besant and Leadbeater as the destined vehicle of the coming World Teacher (Maitreya), head of the Order of the Star in the East founded in his name.
figures.facet: He is the movement's messianic experiment run in public and then repudiated by its own subject — his 1929 dissolution of the Order ('Truth is a pathless land') converts the tradition's central prophecy into its central crisis.
figures.provenance: Historical figure (1895-1986); Mary Lutyens's biographies and Krishnamurti's own 1929 dissolution speech at Ommen.

figures.gloss: The two principal Mahatmas of the founding era, said to have taught Blavatsky directly and to have authored the 'Mahatma Letters' to A. P. Sinnett (1880-1885) explaining the Society's cosmology and purpose.
figures.facet: Named individuals within the generic Mahatma class, they anchor the movement's authority in specific, addressable correspondents rather than an anonymous hierarchy — and became the two most contested figures in Theosophical history after the Society for Psychical Research's 1885 Hodgson Report disputed the letters' authorship.
figures.provenance: 'The Mahatma Letters to A. P. Sinnett' (published 1923); Blavatsky's own attributions in 'The Secret Doctrine'.

figures.gloss: Blavatsky's 1888 two-volume synthesis of cosmogenesis and anthropogenesis, presented as a commentary on the Stanzas of Dzyan and covering the evolution of worlds, root races, and the hierarchy of Masters.
figures.facet: It functions as Theosophy's scripture-equivalent while explicitly refusing the label — a claimed 'synthesis of science, religion and philosophy' whose authority rests on esoteric transmission rather than any single named canon.

figures.gloss: Verses from a claimed archaic 'Book of Dzyan,' written in the secret Senzar tongue, which 'The Secret Doctrine' presents itself as translating and annotating; no manuscript, fragment, or independent attestation of the underlying text has ever been produced.
figures.facet: Its authority is circular by design: the Stanzas validate the Secret Doctrine's cosmology, and the Secret Doctrine is the only evidence the Stanzas exist — a foundational text whose sole witness is its own commentator's clairvoyance.
figures.provenance: Cited throughout Blavatsky's 'The Secret Doctrine' (1888); no independent manuscript source has been located by subsequent scholarship.

figures.gloss: The blissful, self-generated mental state between incarnations in which the ego digests and idealizes the harvest of its most recent life before returning to embodiment; unlike a permanent heaven, it is temporary and dissolves once its content is exhausted.
figures.facet: It re-engineers the Christian afterlife as a subjective processing interval rather than a final destination, keeping reincarnation — not reward — as the actual engine of the system.
figures.provenance: Blavatsky, 'The Key to Theosophy' (1889); elaborated by A. P. Sinnett in 'Esoteric Buddhism' (1883).

figures.gloss: Theosophy's sevenfold anatomy of the human being — dense physical body, etheric double, prana, kama (desire), manas (mind), buddhi, and atma — through which the Monad expresses itself during incarnation.
figures.facet: It supplies the technical scaffolding beneath the Monad and Devachan entries: without naming these seven principles, neither death nor rebirth in the system has any working parts to move through.
figures.provenance: A. P. Sinnett, 'Esoteric Buddhism' (1883); systematized further in Blavatsky's 'The Secret Doctrine' (1888).

edges.note: Parabrahm is the passive absolute source; Fohat is the active, kinetic energy that manifests its cosmic will.
The Mahatmas operate under the direct spiritual authority of Sanat Kumara within the planetary hierarchy.
Lucifer represents the spark of mind that actually ignited the dormant human Monad into self-awareness.
GOLD SEAM: Blavatsky explicitly adopted the Vedantic concept of Brahman as the foundation for her impersonal Absolute.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent the ultimate, unknowable, and limitless divine reality that precedes all cosmic emanation.
GOLD SEAM: Blavatsky specifically identified Fohat with Agni, viewing the Vedic fire god as an allegory for cosmic electricity.
GOLD SEAM: Both are conceptualized as a divine, ethereal matrix containing the fullness of all cosmic blueprints and memories.
GOLD SEAM: Theosophy consciously absorbed the Gnostic framework of an uncreated, divine fragment journeying through matter.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions posit an eternal, indestructible unit of consciousness navigating an immense cycle of physical incarnations.
GOLD SEAM: Blavatsky explicitly conflated Lucifer and Prometheus as identical archetypes of the heroic intellect bringing fire and mind to humanity.
GOLD SEAM: Both are symbolized by the Morning Star (Venus) and function as culture heroes who bring awakening and civilization to mankind.
GOLD SEAM: Both map existence onto unimaginably vast, cyclic timeframes where universes and humanities sequentially emerge and dissolve.
GOLD SEAM: The gradual descent into dense matter followed by an ascent toward spiritualization mirrors the Jain cosmic wheel of time.
GOLD SEAM: The Mahatmas echo the Bodhisattva ideal, delaying their own ultimate liberation to assist the spiritual evolution of humanity.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent the supreme custodians of a primordial, secret wisdom tradition handed down to initiates throughout history.
GOLD SEAM: A cosmic, fiery principle that acts as the active, animating bridge maintaining the vital order of the universe.
GOLD SEAM: Shambhala, the etheric seat of Sanat Kumara, functions as the cosmic center and spiritual axis of the world, echoing Mount Meru.
GOLD SEAM: Both operate as the supreme, overseeing heavenly administrator of the earthly realm, distinct from a monotheistic creator.
Directly descended in name and concept from Blavatsky's Theosophical Akasha, though reframed via Cayce as an individually consultable database rather than a philosophical postulate.
New Age Atlantis/Lemuria lore is a direct, credited descendant of Blavatsky's Third and Fourth Root Races.
Co-founders of the Theosophical Society in New York, 1875.
Blavatsky claimed to write under the direct dictation and instruction of these two Masters.
Author and foundational work.
The Secret Doctrine is structured as translation and commentary on the Stanzas.
Co-developed the Society's later occult cosmology, including 'Thought-Forms' (1901) and the Order of the Star in the East (1911).
Besant headed the Order of the Star in the East and publicly promoted Krishnamurti as the vehicle of the World Teacher.
Leadbeater 'discovered' the boy Krishnamurti at Adyar in 1909 and directed his upbringing as the coming vehicle.
Leadbeater's clairvoyant readings, with Besant, supplied the doctrine of Sanat Kumara and the planetary hierarchy already in this roster.
Co-authored with Leadbeater the later writings establishing Sanat Kumara's role as Lord of the World.
The two named individuals within the generic Mahatma class already rostered.
Devachan is the between-lives resting state through which the reincarnating Monad digests each life's harvest.
Atma-Buddhi, the Monad's own vehicles, are the topmost two of the seven principles.
Steiner headed the Theosophical Society's German Section before founding Anthroposophy in 1912-13, carrying Theosophy's rehabilitated Lucifer doctrine forward into his own cosmology.
Direct doctrinal descent: Anthroposophy split from the Theosophical Society and retained the Akashic Record concept largely intact.
Blavatsky's 'Isis Unveiled' explicitly rejected Spiritualism's communicating ghosts of the dead as unreliable 'shells,' replacing them with living, embodied hidden Adepts as the source of guidance.

El Morya individuates Blavatsky's Mahatma M. of the Mahatma Letters into a discrete Chohan with a ray, retreat, and temperament — the Ascended Master Teachings' devotional personalization of a Theosophical hidden correspondent.
Same name, opposite valence: Blavatsky rehabilitates Christianity's fallen adversary into a principle of awakened mind, naming her own magazine after him.

The I AM/Ascended Master movement (Guy Ballard, 1930s) carried the Leadbeater-era Theosophical Lord of the World wholesale into its own devotional cosmology, adding a Venus-exile rescue narrative absent from Theosophy proper.

Documented direct descent: Guy Ballard's I AM movement promoted Blavatsky's living Himalayan adepts (the Mahatmas, named figures like Morya and Kuthumi) into discarnate, quasi-divine Ascended Masters commanding cosmic decrees.

Blavatsky's Sanat Kumara was already sited on Venus within Theosophical cosmology, easing the Aetherius Society's re-domiciling of the Theosophical Masters as physically resident extraterrestrial intelligences on Venus and Mars.

dossier.essence: The modern esoteric movement founded in New York City in 1875 by Helena Petrovna Blavatsky (1831-1891), Henry Steel Olcott, and William Quan Judge with the establishment of the Theosophical Society, later headquartered at Adyar, India (from 1882). It claims a single 'ancient wisdom-religion' underlying all faiths, preserved and transmitted by hidden adepts, the Mahatmas or 'Masters.' It is the principal taproot of the modern New Age, and its central claim is an evolutionary spiritual science of cosmos and soul held to reconcile religion with science.
dossier.cosmos: An impersonal, attributeless Absolute (Parabrahm) periodically emanates a cosmos that evolves through immense cycles, consciousness descending into matter and reascending across seven 'Root Races' and planetary 'rounds.' Every being is an immortal Monad — a divine spark — making a vast pilgrimage through the mineral, plant, animal, and human kingdoms under the law of karma and reincarnation, guided by a hierarchy of Masters under Sanat Kumara. There is no eternal heaven or hell, only cyclic rebirth toward eventual reunion with the source.
dossier.practice: Theosophy is a matter of study and self-cultivation rather than congregational worship, pursued through lodges and the Society's three stated objects: universal brotherhood without distinction, the comparative study of religion, philosophy, and science, and the investigation of unexplained natural laws and latent human powers. It encourages ethical self-refinement, meditation, and often vegetarianism, with no priesthood or sacrament.
dossier.canon: Blavatsky's 'Isis Unveiled' (1877) and 'The Secret Doctrine' (1888, built on the 'Stanzas of Dzyan'), her 'The Key to Theosophy' and 'The Voice of the Silence' (1889), and the 'Mahatma Letters to A. P. Sinnett,' later systematized by Annie Besant and C. W. Leadbeater.
dossier.reflects: The fusion of religion and science under an evolutionary law — spiritual authority moved from revelation and church to a claimed comparative science of hidden cosmic history.
sources: Olav Hammer, 'Claiming Knowledge: Strategies of Epistemology from Theosophy to the New Age' (2001)
Bruce F. Campbell, 'Ancient Wisdom Revived: A History of the Theosophical Movement' (1980)
